conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the eligibility rules so I can ask you the right questions.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To start, could you please tell me: **How many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible. I'm inquiring about my one child.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for your one child, I need to ask you some questions.

First, could you please tell me **your child's name and age?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: My child's name is Alex and they're 21 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 21 years old, I need to ask an important question:

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** 

(Child Benefit generally stops when a child turns 20, unless there are specific circumstances)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any approved education or training. They actually work full-time now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Based on what you've told me, I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me explain the outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**I'm sorry, but you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you can still claim Child Benefit if they are under 20 **and** staying in approved education or training.

Since Alex is 21 years old and not in approved education or training, they exceed the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility.

If you have any other questions or if your circumstances change in the future, please don't hesitate to ask.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 21 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education; Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right
circumstances: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 21 years old and is not in approved education or training. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you'll normally still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 years old and not in approved education or training, they are over the age limit for Child Benefit eligibility. Therefore, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 21 years old and working full-time, not in approved education or training. As Alex exceeds the maximum age limit of 20 for Child Benefit (which only extends beyond 16 if the child remains in approved education or training), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you'll normally still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 years old and not in approved education or training, they are over the age limit for Child Benefit eligibility. Therefore, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 21 years old and working full-time, not in approved education or training. As Alex exceeds the maximum age limit of 20 for Child Benefit (which only extends beyond 16 if the child remains in approved education or training), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
